hook: You already know how to evaluate simple logical statements like 'It is raining AND I have an umbrella' as true or false once you know the facts. But what if you want to know every possible true/false outcome for a compound statement — without knowing anything about the world yet? For instance, can the statement $(p \land q) \lor \neg p$ ever be false, and for exactly which combinations of $p$ and $q$? You need a systematic tool, and that tool is the truth table.
concept: A truth table is a complete, organized list of every possible assignment of truth values to the propositional variables in a statement, together with the resulting truth value of the whole expression. For a formula built from $n$ distinct variables, the table has $2^n$ rows, one for each combination of T and F. Formally, a truth table for a well-formed formula $\phi(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ is a function from $\{T,F\}^n$ to $\{T,F\}$ that evaluates $\phi$ under every possible interpretation.
anatomy: A truth table has four distinct structural components. First, the **variable columns** — one column per propositional variable (e.g., $p$, $q$, $r$), filled with all $2^n$ binary combinations in a systematic order (usually alternating T/F at halving intervals). Second, the **sub-expression columns** — intermediate columns for parts of the formula such as $\neg p$ or $p \land q$, which break a complex expression into manageable pieces. Third, the **final column**, which carries the truth value of the complete formula for each row and is the ultimate result. Fourth, the **connective evaluation rules** embedded in every step: $\land$ (AND) is T only when both operands are T; $\lor$ (OR) is F only when both are F; $\neg$ (NOT) flips the value; $\rightarrow$ (implication) is F only when the antecedent is T and the consequent is F; $\leftrightarrow$ (biconditional) is T exactly when both sides match.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Build a truth table for } (p \land q) \lor \neg p
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying the variables — there are two, $p$ and $q$ — so we need $2^2 = 4$ rows. We set up columns: $p$, $q$, $\neg p$, $p \land q$, and finally $(p \land q) \lor \neg p$. Working left to right through sub-expressions keeps us organized and prevents errors on the final column.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|c} p & q \\ \hline T & T \\ T & F \\ F & T \\ F & F \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex: We fill the variable columns by cycling $p$ through T, T, F, F and $q$ through T, F, T, F — this alternating pattern guarantees every combination appears exactly once. Notice $p$ changes half as often as $q$; for three variables you would add $r$ cycling T, F, T, F, T, F, T, F.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c} p & q & \neg p \\ \hline T & T & F \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & T \\ F & F & T \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex:
We compute $
eg p$ by flipping each value in the $p$ column: wherever $p$ is T, $
eg p$ is F, and wherever $p$ is F, $
eg p$ is T. This is the simplest connective to evaluate, and getting it right here prevents cascading errors later.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} p & q & \neg p & p \land q \\ \hline T & T & F & T \\ T & F & F & F \\ F & T & T & F \\ F & F & T & F \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex: We evaluate $p \land q$ row by row: AND is T only when both $p$ and $q$ are T, which happens only in row 1. In every other row at least one variable is F, so $p \land q$ is F. This sub-expression column is now ready to be combined with $\neg p$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} p & q & \neg p & p \land q & (p \land q) \lor \neg p \\ \hline T & T & F & T & T \\ T & F & F & F & F \\ F & T & T & F & T \\ F & F & T & F & T \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex: Finally, we compute $(p \land q) \lor \neg p$ by OR-ing the $p \land q$ column with the $\neg p$ column: OR is T whenever at least one operand is T. Row 1: $T \lor F = T$; Row 2: $F \lor F = F$; Row 3: $F \lor T = T$; Row 4: $F \lor T = T$. The formula is false exactly when $p$ is T and $q$ is F — now we have a complete picture of its behavior.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Build a truth table for } p \rightarrow (\neg p \lor q)
partial_example.description_latex:
We have two variables, so we need 4 rows. Set up columns for $p$, $q$, $
eg p$, $
eg p \lor q$, and finally $p ightarrow (
eg p \lor q)$. We fill the variable columns with the standard alternating pattern: $p$ goes T, T, F, F and $q$ goes T, F, T, F.

partial_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} p & q & \neg p & \neg p \lor q \\ \hline T & T & F & T \\ T & F & F & F \\ F & T & T & T \\ F & F & T & T \end{array}
partial_example.description_latex:
We compute $
eg p$ by flipping the $p$ column, then compute $
eg p \lor q$: OR is T unless both operands are F, which only happens in row 2 where $
eg p = F$ and $q = F$. The sub-expression column is now complete and ready for the final step.

Now complete the final column for $p \rightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$. Remember that an implication $A \rightarrow B$ is F only when $A$ is T and $B$ is F — check each row and write T or F for all four rows.

partial_example.expression_latex:
egin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} p & q & 
eg p & 
eg p \lor q & p ightarrow (
eg p \lor q) \ \hline T & T & F & T & T \ T & F & F & F & F \ F & T & T & T & T \ F & F & T & T & T \end{array}
partial_example.description_latex: The completed table shows that $p \rightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ is false only in row 2, where $p$ is T and the sub-expression $\neg p \lor q$ is F. In all other rows the implication holds, so this formula is not a tautology — it fails exactly when $p$ is true and $q$ is false.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Construct a truth table for $p \land \neg p$ and state whether it is a tautology, contradiction, or neither.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c} p & \neg p & p \land \neg p \\ \hline T & F & F \\ F & T & F \end{array} \text{ — contradiction (always F)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex:
Construct a truth table for $
eg(p \lor q)$ and for $
eg p \land 
eg q$. Are they logically equivalent?
practice_problems.answer_latex:
egin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c} p & q & p\lor q & 
eg(p\lor q) & 
eg p & 
eg p \land 
eg q \ \hline T&T&T&F&F&F\ T&F&T&F&F&F\ F&T&T&F&T&F\ F&F&F&T&T&T \end{array} 	ext{ — Yes, identical final columns (De Morgan's Law)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Construct a truth table for $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$ and identify what simpler connective it equals.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} p&q&p\rightarrow q&q\rightarrow p&(p\rightarrow q)\land(q\rightarrow p)\\ \hline T&T&T&T&T\\ T&F&F&T&F\\ F&T&T&F&F\\ F&F&T&T&T \end{array} \text{ — equivalent to } p \leftrightarrow q

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Using a truth table, determine for which truth values of $p$, $q$, $r$ the formula $p \land (q \lor r)$ has a different truth value from $(p \land q) \lor r$. (Hint: you need $2^3 = 8$ rows.)
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{The two formulas differ in rows where } p=F, r=T \text{ (i.e., rows 5 and 7 in standard order): } p \land(q\lor r)=F \text{ but } (p\land q)\lor r=T \text{ there. They are NOT equivalent.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A security system triggers an alarm ($A$) according to the rule: 'The alarm sounds if and only if the motion sensor ($m$) is activated or the door sensor ($d$) is activated, but not both.' Write this as $A \leftrightarrow (m \oplus d)$ where $\oplus$ denotes XOR ($m \oplus d$ is T when exactly one of $m$, $d$ is T). Build its truth table and find all input combinations that set $A = T$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} m&d&m\oplus d&A\leftrightarrow(m\oplus d)\\ \hline T&T&F&F\\ T&F&T&T\\ F&T&T&T\\ F&F&F&T \end{array} \text{ — } A=T \text{ when } (m=T,d=F),\,(m=F,d=T), \text{ or }(m=F,d=F)

common_mistakes: Students misread the implication rule and mark $p \rightarrow q$ as F whenever $p$ is F. In fact, a conditional is only F when the antecedent $p$ is T and the consequent $q$ is F; any row where $p$ is F automatically gives $p \rightarrow q = T$ regardless of $q$.

Students skip intermediate sub-expression columns and try to evaluate a complex formula in one step, leading to operator precedence errors — for example, evaluating $p \lor q \land r$ as $(p \lor q) \land r$ instead of $p \lor (q \land r)$ because $\land$ binds more tightly than $\lor$. Always add a separate column for each sub-expression and apply the precedence order: $\neg$ first, then $\land$, then $\lor$, then $\rightarrow$, then $\leftrightarrow$.

Students fill the variable columns in an ad-hoc order and accidentally repeat or omit a combination of truth values, making the table incomplete. The fix is to always use the standard systematic pattern: the rightmost variable alternates T/F every row, the next variable alternates every 2 rows, the next every 4 rows, and so on, guaranteeing all $2^n$ distinct rows appear exactly once.
untested_variants: Three-variable truth tables involving all five connectives simultaneously (e.g., $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg q \lor r)$), which require 8-row tables and careful precedence handling.
Using truth tables to test the validity of a logical argument form, such as verifying that $[(p \rightarrow q) \land p] \rightarrow q$ (modus ponens) is a tautology.

Constructing a truth table for a formula containing the exclusive-or connective $\oplus$ in a purely abstract setting (not a word problem), then using it to prove or disprove equivalence with another formula.
Reading a given completed truth table and reverse-engineering (reconstructing) the logical formula in disjunctive normal form (DNF) that matches exactly those rows marked T.
